meta.title: DBX-CEA-4: Prompt Hierarchy, Retrieval Precision, and Evaluation Signals

questions.stem: Which statement BEST describes what must be managed within a model's context window in a production agent?

options.text: Only the most recent user message counts toward the context window limit
options.explanation: All components of the prompt contribute to context token usage, not just the latest user message.

options.text: The system prompt, conversation history, retrieved chunks, tool call inputs and outputs, and the current user message all consume context tokens
options.explanation: Every element passed to the model — system prompt, history, retrieved context, tool traces, and the new user message — competes for the same finite context window budget.

options.text: Context limits apply only to output tokens, not input tokens
options.explanation: Both input and output tokens count toward context limits; input tokens fill the context window and output tokens are counted separately against the max output limit.

options.text: SQL warehouse result caches eliminate context window constraints for production agents
options.explanation: Result caches store query outputs and have no effect on model context limits.

questions.stem: When are few-shot examples most useful in a system prompt?

options.text: When the task requires a consistent transformation pattern or output style
options.explanation: Examples are most valuable when they show a repeatable input-output pattern the model should imitate—such as a structured extraction schema or a specific rewriting style—because demonstrating is more reliable than describing.

options.text: When the engineer wants to maximize prompt length regardless of cost
options.explanation: Deliberately padding prompt length without task benefit wastes token budget and can dilute the model's attention to the instructions that matter.

options.text: Only when the agent never uses retrieval
options.explanation: Few-shot examples are not mutually exclusive with retrieval; many RAG systems include examples in the system prompt alongside retrieved passages.

options.text: Only for image generation workloads
options.explanation: Few-shot examples apply broadly across text, code, and structured-output tasks, not only to image generation.

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY value of defining a well-structured tool schema (name, description, parameters with types) for an agent?

options.text: It guarantees the model will never produce incorrect output when the tool is used
options.explanation: A schema improves reliability but does not guarantee correct outputs; the model can still reason incorrectly about when or how to use the tool.

options.text: It allows the model to understand the tool's purpose and call signature so it can invoke the tool correctly
options.explanation: A clear schema with names, descriptions, and typed parameters gives the model the information it needs to decide when and how to call the tool correctly.

options.text: It replaces authentication and access-control mechanisms for the tool
options.explanation: Authentication and governance are infrastructure concerns separate from the schema definition.

options.text: It eliminates the need for system prompt instructions that mention the tool
options.explanation: System prompt instructions about when and why to use tools complement the schema and should not be eliminated.

questions.stem: A financial advisory agent must distinguish between facts the user stated in the current session versus facts learned in previous sessions. Which design supports this distinction MOST reliably?

options.text: Store all session facts inline in the chat history without any labeling or tagging
options.explanation: Unlabeled inline facts give the model no signal about recency or source, making it harder to apply appropriate confidence or staleness checks.

options.text: Maintain separate memory scopes: a labeled in-session scratchpad for current-turn facts and a labeled long-term profile for cross-session facts
options.explanation: Distinct labeled memory scopes let the model and the engineer apply different confidence levels, update frequencies, and staleness logic to in-session versus long-term facts.

options.text: Use a single flat memory block and rely on the model to infer fact provenance
options.explanation: A flat block without scope labels requires the model to infer provenance, which it cannot do reliably.

options.text: Retrieve the full user history on every query regardless of relevance to avoid missing any detail
options.explanation: Retrieving all history on every query wastes context tokens and may surface stale or irrelevant information.

questions.stem: An agent must always respond in JSON with fields `answer` and `citations`. Where should that requirement be placed for the MOST consistent behavior?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instructions
options.explanation: Persistent formatting and behavioral constraints belong in the system-level instructions, which govern every turn of the conversation regardless of retrieved content or user phrasing.

options.text: Only in the final user message of each session
options.explanation: Relying on the final user message means the constraint can be missing or inconsistently worded across sessions.

options.text: Inside retrieved document chunks
options.explanation: Retrieved chunks are variable and cannot reliably enforce output format on every response.

options.text: In a SQL warehouse column comment
options.explanation: Warehouse column comments are metadata annotations, not agent instruction surfaces.

questions.stem: An agent's conversation history is growing so large that it causes context overflow. The agent still needs to act on decisions made in earlier turns. Which technique BEST addresses this?

options.text: Delete all history after every message to keep the context short
options.explanation: Deleting all history loses prior decisions the agent needs for coherent multi-turn behavior.

options.text: Summarize older turns into a compact state representation and retain only recent verbatim exchanges
options.explanation: Summarization compresses older turns into a dense state description, preserving decision-relevant information while dramatically reducing token usage.

options.text: Duplicate the full transcript in both the system prompt and the user message
options.explanation: Duplicating the transcript worsens the overflow problem by consuming more tokens.

options.text: Move the entire conversation history into Unity Catalog column comments
options.explanation: Unity Catalog column comments are metadata for data assets, not a conversational memory mechanism.

questions.stem: A data team wants agents to call governed data logic rather than ad hoc SQL. Which Databricks feature best matches that goal?

options.text: Unity Catalog functions exposed as governed tools
options.explanation: Unity Catalog functions encapsulate business logic with defined inputs and outputs, are versioned and access-controlled through Unity Catalog, and can be registered as agent tools so calls go through approved, auditable logic rather than arbitrary SQL.

options.text: Notebook markdown cells
options.explanation: Notebook markdown cells are static documentation and cannot be invoked as executable logic by an agent tool call.

options.text: Cluster-scoped environment variables
options.explanation: Environment variables configure runtime settings for a cluster and are not callable data logic; they do not enforce the data governance or versioning that Unity Catalog functions provide.

options.text: Driver logs
options.explanation: Driver logs are diagnostic output from Spark job execution and are not callable functions; they provide observability, not executable governed logic.

questions.stem: What is the main architectural benefit of separating an orchestrator agent from specialist sub-agents?

options.text: It can route tasks to narrower context and tools instead of forcing one agent to handle everything
options.explanation: An orchestrator can decompose a complex task and dispatch each sub-task to a specialist agent whose context window, tools, and instructions are optimized for that narrow domain, improving accuracy and reducing irrelevant tool exposure.

options.text: It removes the need for prompts
options.explanation: Every agent in a multi-agent system still requires its own prompt to define its role, constraints, and tool permissions; orchestration adds routing logic but does not eliminate prompting.

options.text: It guarantees lower cost in every workload
options.explanation: Multi-agent architectures introduce additional LLM calls for routing and sub-agent execution; cost depends on workload complexity and may be higher or lower than a single-agent approach.

options.text: It makes retrieval unnecessary
options.explanation: Specialist sub-agents often rely on their own retrieval tools to ground responses in domain-specific knowledge; multi-agent design does not remove the need for retrieval.

questions.stem: An agent using the Model Context Protocol (MCP) needs to expose a company's internal knowledge base as a resource server. What does MCP's resource abstraction primarily allow?

options.text: MCP resources allow a server to expose structured or unstructured content that a host agent can read into context without embedding retrieval logic in the agent itself
options.explanation: MCP's resource abstraction lets a server expose any content (files, database rows, API responses) as named resources that a host can fetch and inject into context, decoupling retrieval implementation from agent logic.

options.text: MCP resources replace the need for a vector index by storing all documents inside the protocol layer
options.explanation: MCP is a protocol for exposing capabilities; the underlying storage and retrieval (e.g., a vector index) still exists on the server side.

options.text: MCP resources are exclusively used for streaming audio and video media content
options.explanation: MCP resources are a general-purpose abstraction for any content type, not specifically for media streams.

options.text: MCP resources automatically summarize and compress documents before returning them to the agent
options.explanation: MCP resources deliver content as-is; summarization would need to be implemented explicitly on the server.

questions.stem: An engineering team stores all company policies as a vector index. At query time, the top-3 relevant policy chunks are inserted into the system prompt. What is the PRIMARY risk of this design?

options.text: Retrieval failures or low similarity scores may cause critical policies to be omitted from the prompt
options.explanation: Dynamically retrieved policies depend on retrieval quality. If the query is phrased unusually or the embedding similarity is low, the relevant policy may not appear in the top-3, leaving the agent without critical constraints.

options.text: The vector index will automatically update the system prompt at training time
options.explanation: Vector indexes influence inference-time prompts, not model training weights.

options.text: Using top-3 retrieval always returns too many tokens for any model
options.explanation: Whether top-3 is too many tokens depends on chunk size and is not universally true; this is not the primary risk.

options.text: Vector search cannot be integrated with system prompts
options.explanation: Vector search can absolutely be integrated with dynamic system prompt construction.

questions.stem: A RAG agent is returning answers that cite irrelevant paragraphs. The retrieval logs show high similarity scores for the wrong chunks. What is the MOST likely root cause?

options.text: The embedding model is misaligned with the domain vocabulary; a domain-adapted embedding model would better capture relevance
options.explanation: High similarity scores for wrong chunks indicate the embedding space is not aligned with domain-specific meaning. Specialized or fine-tuned embedding models better represent domain vocabulary and improve retrieval precision.

options.text: The SQL warehouse is auto-stopping too quickly between requests
options.explanation: Warehouse auto-stop affects compute availability, not vector similarity scoring.

options.text: The agent's generation temperature is too low
options.explanation: Temperature is a generation parameter and does not influence which documents are retrieved.

options.text: The Unity Catalog permissions are too restrictive for the embedding service
options.explanation: Unity Catalog permissions control access, not similarity ranking.

questions.stem: When would importance-based memory retrieval be preferable to simple recency-only replay?

options.text: When older facts can still be critical even if they were mentioned many turns ago
options.explanation: Recency-only replay discards older messages as the window fills; if a critical constraint or user preference was stated early in a long conversation, importance-based retrieval re-surfaces it even if it would otherwise be pruned.

options.text: When no memory store exists
options.explanation: Importance-based retrieval requires a memory store to read from; without one, neither strategy has anything to retrieve.

options.text: Only when the conversation has one turn
options.explanation: A one-turn conversation has no history to manage; importance-based retrieval is only meaningful when there is enough conversational context to require selective selection.

options.text: When the application never needs personalization
options.explanation: Personalization is a primary motivation for importance-based memory; an application that never needs personalization has less need for this approach, not more.

questions.stem: An agent's context window overflows after 8 turns because tool call traces from prior steps are never pruned. Which strategy MOST directly solves this?

options.text: Prune or summarize completed tool traces after the agent has processed their results, retaining only the key outcome
options.explanation: Completed tool traces whose results have already been incorporated can be compressed to just their outcome (e.g., 'searched for X, found Y'), dramatically reducing token accumulation across long workflows.

options.text: Increase the model's generation temperature to shorten output length
options.explanation: Temperature is a generation parameter and does not affect how many tokens tool traces consume in the input.

options.text: Store tool traces in a Unity Catalog table so they are not in the prompt
options.explanation: Storing traces externally still requires fetching and injecting them into context if needed; the token cost is not reduced by their storage location alone.

options.text: Disable tool calling after turn 4 to prevent further trace accumulation
options.explanation: Disabling tool calling removes agent capability as a workaround rather than addressing the token accumulation problem.

questions.stem: Which memory pattern is most appropriate for storing a user's preferred output language across sessions?

options.text: Long-term semantic or profile memory
options.explanation: A language preference is a stable user attribute that must survive session boundaries; long-term profile memory persists it in durable storage so it can be retrieved at the start of any future session.

options.text: A temporary local Python variable that disappears after the turn
options.explanation: A Python variable lives only in the current process execution scope; once the turn ends or the process exits, the preference is lost.

options.text: A one-time notebook cell result
options.explanation: A notebook cell result is transient output from a single notebook execution and is not accessible to an agent across different sessions or requests.

options.text: A random embedding vector with no metadata
options.explanation: An embedding vector without associated metadata cannot be identified or queried as a preference; retrieving it would require knowing which vector corresponds to which user's language setting.

questions.stem: Which TWO examples best represent long-term agent memory rather than temporary in-turn context? (Select TWO)

options.text: A stored preference profile retrieved across future sessions
options.explanation: A preference profile is written to durable storage after a session and retrieved at the start of future sessions, surviving beyond any single context window.

options.text: A persisted record of previous task outcomes used later for personalization
options.explanation: Persisted task outcome records are stored externally and retrieved selectively for personalization in later interactions, which is the defining characteristic of long-term memory.

options.text: The system prompt for the current request only
options.explanation: The system prompt is assembled fresh for each request; it is part of the current turn's context window and discarded when the turn ends.

options.text: The last two user messages that are still inside the context window
options.explanation: Messages inside the current context window are by definition short-term, in-turn context; they do not persist across session boundaries.

questions.stem: A user asks about EU-only support policies. Which retrieval refinement is most helpful before generation?

options.text: Apply a metadata filter for region or document scope
options.explanation: A metadata filter scoped to region=EU or document_type=support_policy ensures that the dense similarity search only considers EU-specific content, preventing global or US-only policy passages from polluting the retrieved context.

options.text: Increase temperature
options.explanation: Temperature is a generation hyperparameter that controls output randomness; increasing it would make the answer less predictable, not more regionally accurate.

options.text: Delete citations
options.explanation: Deleting citations removes provenance from the answer, making it harder for users to verify regional scope; it does not improve the relevance of retrieved passages.

options.text: Force a longer answer
options.explanation: Forcing answer length is a generation instruction that does not change which source documents are retrieved; the model would simply produce a longer response from whatever passages were already selected.

questions.stem: Which signal is most useful when validating that a retrieval system is missing important documents?

options.text: Low recall on queries with known relevant passages
options.explanation: Recall measures the fraction of known-relevant passages that the retrieval system returns; low recall on a labeled test set directly identifies that important documents are being missed.

options.text: The assistant has a short name
options.explanation: The length of the assistant's display name has no relationship to retrieval system performance.

options.text: A larger browser window
options.explanation: Browser window size is a UI setting unrelated to the recall or precision of a vector search index.

options.text: The warehouse uses SQL
options.explanation: The query language used by the warehouse does not indicate whether the retrieval system is returning all relevant passages.

questions.stem: A tool returns a 30,000-token raw payload, but the agent needs only five fields. What is the best design improvement?

options.text: Have the tool return or post-process into a compact structured summary with the needed fields
options.explanation: Filtering the tool response to only the required five fields at the tool layer—before it enters the context window—reduces a 30,000-token payload to a fraction of that cost without losing any information the agent actually needs.

options.text: Increase the prompt size until the full payload fits
options.explanation: Expanding the prompt to accommodate oversized tool outputs treats a design problem as a capacity problem; it increases cost and latency on every turn and is unsustainable as payloads grow.

options.text: Ask users to read the raw payload themselves
options.explanation: An agent-based system is automated; offloading raw payload inspection to users removes the value of the agent and is not a viable production design.

options.text: Store the payload in the system prompt forever
options.explanation: Embedding the payload in the system prompt makes it a permanent, non-refreshable part of every request, consuming token budget even when the data is stale or irrelevant to the current query.

questions.stem: Which TWO retrieval improvements most directly reduce the chance that a relevant passage is buried below a near-duplicate but less useful result? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply reranking after initial retrieval
options.explanation: A cross-encoder reranker re-scores the initial candidate set with full query-passage attention, often surfacing a more relevant passage that a fast ANN search ranked poorly.

options.text: Use maximal marginal relevance or similar diversity-aware ranking
options.explanation: Maximal marginal relevance penalizes candidates that are semantically similar to already-selected results, promoting diversity and preventing near-duplicate blocks from crowding out distinct relevant passages.

options.text: Remove metadata from the index entirely
options.explanation: Removing metadata eliminates the ability to apply attribute filters that narrow the result set, making diversity and precision harder to achieve, not easier.

options.text: Append every document into one single chunk
options.explanation: Merging all documents into one chunk destroys retrieval granularity; the single embedding represents everything and matches nothing precisely, making it impossible to surface the specific relevant passage.

questions.stem: What is the main risk of trimming context only by recency with no importance checks?

options.text: A critical older constraint may be removed even though it still matters
options.explanation: Recency-only trimming discards the oldest messages first; if a user stated a critical constraint or preference early in a long conversation, that information is removed even if it still governs every subsequent response.

options.text: The model gains more parameters
options.explanation: Model parameters are fixed weights set during training; context trimming strategy at inference time does not alter the model's parameter count.

options.text: Vector Search stops working permanently
options.explanation: Vector Search is an independent service; how the application trims its context window has no effect on the availability or function of the retrieval index.

options.text: The application can no longer authenticate
options.explanation: Application authentication is managed through credentials and identity tokens at the infrastructure layer, not through prompt context management decisions.

questions.stem: Which TWO controls most directly reduce the risk of sensitive data being passed into prompts? (Select TWO)

options.text: Apply policy checks before retrieval or tool output is inserted into context
options.explanation: A policy check at the context-assembly stage intercepts sensitive content before it enters the prompt, preventing it from being processed by or leaked through the model regardless of what the retrieval step returned.

options.text: Tag and classify sensitive assets in the metadata layer
options.explanation: Tagging and classifying sensitive assets in Unity Catalog metadata is the prerequisite for enforcement; without classification, policy checks cannot identify which content is sensitive and must be blocked.

options.text: Ask the model to ignore privacy requirements
options.explanation: Instructing the model to ignore privacy requirements is the opposite of risk reduction; the model may still surface sensitive data in its responses and the instruction itself is not a reliable enforcement mechanism.

options.text: Allow unrestricted access to all catalogs for convenience
options.explanation: Granting unrestricted catalog access maximizes the volume of potentially sensitive data reachable by the agent, directly increasing the risk of sensitive content appearing in prompts.

questions.stem: What is graceful degradation when context would overflow?

options.text: Reducing lower-priority context and informing the user or workflow when necessary
options.explanation: Graceful degradation means the system responds to an impending overflow by systematically shedding the lowest-priority content (old messages, less-relevant chunks) and, when necessary, notifying the user that some context was condensed, so behavior degrades predictably rather than failing.

options.text: Ignoring the model limit and sending everything
options.explanation: Ignoring the model limit and sending an oversized prompt will cause a hard error or silent truncation at the API level, resulting in unpredictable behavior, not graceful handling.

options.text: Deleting all safety instructions
options.explanation: Safety instructions are the highest-priority content; deleting them first is the opposite of graceful degradation and leaves the agent unguarded.

options.text: Returning a random answer without evidence
options.explanation: Returning an unsupported answer without evidence is a failure mode, not a degradation strategy; the goal is to still produce a grounded, if less complete, response.

questions.stem: An engineering team wants every agent reply to include a short answer plus a citations array. Where should that durable instruction live for the most reliable behavior?

options.text: In the system prompt or equivalent top-level instruction layer
options.explanation: The system prompt is processed on every turn and carries the highest instruction priority, making it the reliable home for durable output requirements like format contracts.

options.text: Only in the latest user message
options.explanation: User messages vary turn by turn and the model may not apply a format instruction from a single prior turn to all subsequent responses.

options.text: Inside the retrieved passages
options.explanation: Retrieved passages are dynamic, per-query content whose presence is not guaranteed; burying format rules there means they are silently absent when retrieval returns different chunks.

options.text: Inside an MLflow experiment tag
options.explanation: MLflow experiment tags are metadata for tracking experiments and have no influence on the model's output format at inference time.

questions.stem: An agent's system prompt currently reads: "You are a helpful, friendly, knowledgeable, professional, accurate, concise, and thoughtful assistant that always tries to do its best to help users and never gives up." Which critique BEST applies?

options.text: The prompt is too short and needs more adjectives to be effective
options.explanation: More adjectives would make the problem worse, not better.

options.text: The adjective list provides no actionable constraints and wastes tokens without changing behavior
options.explanation: Stacking vague adjectives does not give the model actionable constraints. Context engineering best practices favor specific, testable behavioral rules over personality descriptors.

options.text: The prompt should list adjectives in alphabetical order for clarity
options.explanation: Alphabetical ordering is irrelevant to instruction effectiveness.

options.text: The prompt is well-formed and follows context engineering best practices
options.explanation: This prompt violates context engineering principles by consuming tokens on decorative persona text with no measurable behavioral impact.

questions.stem: An engineering team is integrating external REST APIs as agent tools. Select TWO practices that BEST improve reliability and debuggability. (Select TWO)

options.text: Validate tool call inputs against the declared parameter schema before sending the API request
options.explanation: Pre-call schema validation prevents malformed requests from reaching the API, reducing errors and wasted latency.

options.text: Return structured error responses with actionable messages so the agent can retry or ask for clarification
options.explanation: Structured error responses give the model enough context to recover, retry with corrected parameters, or surface a meaningful message to the user.

options.text: Suppress all tool call logs to minimize storage overhead
options.explanation: Suppressing logs removes the observability needed to diagnose failures in production.

options.text: Accept all inputs as a single untyped free-text string to simplify the schema
options.explanation: A single free-text string provides no type safety or schema guidance, making it harder for the model to construct correct calls.

questions.stem: Why summarize earlier turns instead of keeping the full transcript forever?

options.text: To manage token budget while preserving the most important information
options.explanation: The context window has a fixed token limit; summarizing older turns compresses low-density history into a compact representation that preserves key facts while leaving room for new turns, retrieved passages, and tool outputs.

options.text: To increase hallucinations
options.explanation: Summarization aims to preserve accuracy by retaining the key facts; well-implemented summarization reduces hallucination risk rather than increasing it.

options.text: To disable tool use
options.explanation: Summarizing conversation history does not affect the agent's ability to call tools; tool availability is determined by the tool definitions provided to the model.

options.text: To remove all user preferences from the conversation
options.explanation: A good summarization strategy explicitly preserves important user preferences and constraints; removing them is a defect in the summarization design, not its purpose.

questions.stem: What does a regression test set help detect after retrieval or prompt changes?

options.text: Whether previously solved scenarios now perform worse
options.explanation: A regression test set contains queries whose correct answers were previously validated; running the updated system against this set reveals whether a retrieval or prompt change degraded performance on already-solved scenarios.

options.text: Whether the warehouse changed node type
options.explanation: Warehouse node type is a compute infrastructure attribute; it can affect query latency but is not something a model quality regression test set detects.

options.text: Whether the UI theme is different
options.explanation: UI theme changes are frontend presentation concerns entirely unrelated to model quality evaluation.

options.text: Whether users renamed a notebook
options.explanation: Notebook renaming is a workspace management action with no bearing on whether the agent's answers on a regression test set have changed quality.

questions.stem: Why tag context sources with quality or sensitivity metadata in Unity Catalog?

options.text: So retrieval and policy logic can prefer trusted sources and restrict sensitive content
options.explanation: Quality and sensitivity tags attached to tables or columns in Unity Catalog enable retrieval filters and access policies to route queries only to high-trust sources and block sensitive data from being included in prompts for unauthorized users.

options.text: So embeddings are no longer needed
options.explanation: Metadata tags are attribute labels stored alongside data assets; they do not affect the embedding pipeline, which still produces vector representations for semantic search.

options.text: So every user automatically becomes an admin
options.explanation: Unity Catalog tag creation and assignment does not alter user permission levels; privilege escalation requires explicit grants through IAM or Unity Catalog access controls.

options.text: So prompt length always decreases
options.explanation: Tagging is a metadata operation; it does not compress or shorten the retrieved text that is ultimately inserted into the prompt.

questions.stem: A governance review is focused on preventing sensitive PII from entering agent prompts. Select TWO controls that BEST align with this goal. (Select TWO)

options.text: Classify sensitive data sources in Unity Catalog and restrict retrieval tool access by classification tag
options.explanation: Classifying data sources in Unity Catalog and enforcing tag-based access controls prevents the retrieval tool from fetching sensitive documents in the first place.

options.text: Apply column masking policies so PII fields are redacted before being returned to the agent
options.explanation: Column masking policies redact PII at the data layer before results reach the agent, ensuring sensitive values never enter the context window.

options.text: Increase top-k retrieval to maximize coverage and reduce the chance of missing relevant records
options.explanation: Increasing top-k widens retrieval and is more likely to surface sensitive records, not less.

options.text: Require all agent responses to include a data privacy disclaimer footer
options.explanation: Response disclaimers do not prevent PII from entering the prompt; they only affect what is shown to the user after the fact.

questions.stem: What problem is semantic chunking designed to address compared with naive fixed-size chunks?

options.text: It groups content by meaning boundaries so chunks keep coherent ideas together
options.explanation: Fixed-size chunking splits at arbitrary character counts and can cut mid-sentence or mid-concept; semantic chunking detects topic or sentence-group boundaries so each chunk contains a coherent unit of meaning, improving embedding quality and retrieval relevance.

options.text: It disables vector indexing
options.explanation: Semantic chunking produces chunks that are still embedded and stored in a vector index; it does not disable vector indexing.

options.text: It guarantees lower storage cost than any other method
options.explanation: Semantic chunking may produce variable-size chunks that in aggregate use similar or more storage than fixed-size chunks; storage efficiency is not its primary goal.

options.text: It makes BM25 impossible to use
options.explanation: BM25 keyword search can be applied to any text chunks regardless of how they were segmented; semantic chunking does not affect keyword search availability.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the MOST direct consequence of exhausting a model's context window during a multi-turn conversation?

options.text: The model automatically switches to a larger model tier to accommodate additional tokens
options.explanation: Context overflow does not trigger automatic model upgrades; that is a separate infrastructure decision made by the operator.

options.text: Older content must be dropped or the request will fail, potentially losing important context
options.explanation: The context window is a hard limit. When exceeded, the application must either truncate or summarize older content, or the API call will fail with a token limit error — either outcome risks losing important conversational context.

options.text: The model dynamically compresses its weights at inference time to fit more tokens
options.explanation: Model weights are fixed after training and cannot be dynamically compressed at inference time.

options.text: The SQL warehouse increases its memory allocation to compensate for the overflow
options.explanation: SQL warehouse memory and model context windows are independent systems with no connection.

questions.stem: Which TWO strategies usually preserve answer quality while reducing prompt size? (Select TWO)

options.text: Summarize older resolved discussion threads
options.explanation: Summarizing older resolved threads replaces verbose turn-by-turn exchange with a compact representation that preserves the key outcome, freeing space for current context without losing important history.

options.text: Filter retrieval to the most relevant chunks and metadata constraints
options.explanation: Metadata-constrained retrieval reduces the candidate pool before similarity scoring, ensuring fewer but more relevant chunks are included in the prompt, directly lowering token usage while improving grounding quality.

options.text: Append duplicate copies of the same policy manual
options.explanation: Duplicating the same policy manual multiplies token consumption by the number of copies without adding any new information, wasting context budget.

options.text: Replace all structure with a single unformatted paragraph
options.explanation: Removing structure such as JSON keys, section headers, and bullet points makes it harder for the model to locate specific fields and reasons, hurting reasoning quality rather than preserving it.

questions.stem: A Vector Search query filters by `department = 'finance'` before performing semantic search. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this approach?

options.text: Pre-filtering restricts the candidate pool to relevant documents, reducing noise in retrieved results and improving precision
options.explanation: Metadata pre-filtering scopes the similarity search to a relevant subset, preventing cross-department documents from occupying top-k slots and improving both precision and context quality.

options.text: Pre-filtering replaces the need for embeddings entirely
options.explanation: Pre-filtering narrows the search space but does not replace the semantic similarity computation over the filtered set.

options.text: Pre-filtering increases the total number of results returned
options.explanation: Pre-filtering constrains the candidate pool; it typically reduces, not increases, the number of potential results.

options.text: Pre-filtering eliminates latency because no vector comparison is performed
options.explanation: Vector comparison is still performed over the filtered subset; latency is reduced but not eliminated.

questions.stem: A team observes that their agent's response quality degrades when the retrieved context section exceeds 6,000 tokens even though the model supports 100k tokens total. What phenomenon BEST explains this?

options.text: Models have difficulty attending to information buried in the middle of a very long context, sometimes called the 'lost in the middle' effect
options.explanation: Research shows that transformer models tend to attend more reliably to information at the beginning and end of a long context, with material in the middle receiving less attention — this degrades answer quality when critical context is buried in the middle of a large retrieval block.

options.text: Retrieved context above 6,000 tokens automatically triggers an expensive model fine-tuning job
options.explanation: Retrieval context length does not trigger fine-tuning; fine-tuning is a separate offline training process.

options.text: The SQL warehouse throttles API requests that contain more than 6,000 tokens of payload
options.explanation: SQL warehouse throttling is related to compute resources, not the content length of API payloads.

options.text: Longer retrieved contexts force the model to use a smaller output vocabulary
options.explanation: Vocabulary size is fixed by the tokenizer and does not change based on context length.

questions.stem: An engineering team wants to continuously monitor a deployed RAG agent for answer quality degradation as the retrieval index is updated weekly. Which approach BEST enables this?

options.text: Run a fixed evaluation benchmark against the agent after each index update and track quality metrics over time in MLflow
options.explanation: Running a consistent evaluation benchmark after each index change and tracking metrics in MLflow creates a time-series view of quality, enabling automated alerts when metrics degrade beyond a defined threshold.

options.text: Manually review 10 randomly selected answers each week and make a subjective judgment call
options.explanation: 10 random answers provide insufficient statistical power to detect subtle degradation, and the process is too manual to be operationally sustainable at scale.

options.text: Block all index updates until a dedicated QA team individually approves each new document
options.explanation: Blocking all index updates creates operational bottlenecks and does not scale with frequent content changes.

options.text: Monitor only API error rates as a proxy for overall answer quality
options.explanation: API error rates measure service availability, not answer quality; an agent can have 100% uptime and still produce degraded answers.

questions.stem: MLflow is used to track an agent's experiment runs across a multi-step evaluation workflow. What is the PRIMARY benefit of using MLflow for this purpose?

options.text: MLflow provides reproducible run tracking with logged parameters, metrics, and artifacts that allow state to be inspected and replayed
options.explanation: MLflow's run tracking records parameters, metrics, and artifacts with timestamps, enabling inspection, debugging, and reproducibility of agent behavior across steps.

options.text: MLflow automatically summarizes conversation history into compact prompts
options.explanation: MLflow does not automatically summarize conversation history; it stores what is explicitly logged by the application.

options.text: MLflow stores embeddings in a vector index for semantic retrieval of past runs
options.explanation: MLflow is a model lifecycle platform; vector storage and retrieval are handled by Databricks Vector Search.

options.text: MLflow generates the system prompt automatically from past run metadata
options.explanation: MLflow does not generate system prompts; prompt management is the engineer's responsibility.

questions.stem: An engineering team stores every conversation message forever and always replays all of them to the model. What is the main downside?

options.text: It usually increases cost and latency while making the prompt harder to control
options.explanation: Replaying the full unbounded transcript inflates token count every turn, directly increasing inference cost and latency, and buries the most relevant recent context under a long history of older, lower-value messages.

options.text: It guarantees better grounding
options.explanation: Replaying everything does not guarantee better grounding; old or contradictory messages can confuse the model and older facts that have been superseded may steer incorrect responses.

options.text: It removes the need for retrieval
options.explanation: Replaying full transcripts provides conversation history, not domain knowledge; a retrieval system over a knowledge corpus still serves a distinct and necessary function.

options.text: It prevents prompt injection entirely
options.explanation: Prompt injection is an adversarial attack vector where malicious text in user input or retrieved content hijacks model behavior; it is not prevented by accumulating transcript history.

questions.stem: A team builds a RAG pipeline that retrieves the top-20 chunks and passes all of them to the model. Users report slow responses and sometimes incoherent answers. Which improvement is MOST targeted?

options.text: Reduce top-k to a smaller set of the most relevant chunks and apply a re-ranker to ensure quality
options.explanation: Passing 20 chunks floods the context window with low-relevance material, increasing latency and diluting signal. Reducing top-k with re-ranking provides fewer, higher-quality chunks.

options.text: Increase the vector index size to allow more candidate documents
options.explanation: A larger index would increase the pool of candidates, not reduce the number passed to the model.

options.text: Switch to a SQL-only retrieval strategy with no embeddings
options.explanation: SQL retrieval retrieves structured rows, not unstructured document chunks, and does not solve the chunk-volume problem.

options.text: Move all retrieved chunks into the system prompt section unchanged
options.explanation: Moving chunks to the system prompt section does not reduce their count or improve relevance.

questions.stem: A team is building a coding assistant. They want the agent to explain its reasoning before giving code, but only for requests where the user explicitly asks 'why'. Which instruction strategy is BEST?

options.text: Always include chain-of-thought reasoning in every response regardless of user request
options.explanation: Unconditional chain-of-thought adds tokens on every request, consuming budget when the user does not need it.

options.text: Provide a conditional rule: generate an explanation section only when the user's message contains a 'why' question
options.explanation: Conditional instructions tie context assembly to actual need, allowing the agent to spend tokens on reasoning only when it provides value to the user.

options.text: Remove all reasoning from responses to save tokens unconditionally
options.explanation: Removing all reasoning degrades output quality on questions that benefit from structured thinking.

options.text: Store the reasoning requirement in a separate database table and query it at runtime
options.explanation: A runtime database lookup for a simple conditional instruction adds unnecessary latency and architectural complexity.

questions.stem: A conversation is approaching the model's limit. What is usually the best response?

options.text: Summarize or trim lower-value context while preserving the instructions and critical facts
options.explanation: Graceful compaction—summarizing resolved sub-topics, dropping redundant messages, or pruning low-relevance retrieved passages—preserves the highest-signal content and keeps the prompt within limits without losing critical instructions or facts.

options.text: Add all remaining documents anyway
options.explanation: Inserting more content when the context is nearly full will exceed the model limit, causing errors or hard truncation that may remove important content silently.

options.text: Delete the system prompt first
options.explanation: The system prompt contains the governance rules and output format contracts; deleting it first removes the highest-priority instructions and degrades agent behavior most severely.

options.text: Convert the answer to an image
options.explanation: Converting the answer format does not reduce prompt size; it is the input prompt, not the output response type, that is the binding constraint.

questions.stem: Which prompt-writing practice usually improves maintainability for a production agent?

options.text: Mixing role, constraints, and examples into one long paragraph
options.explanation: Mixing all concerns into one block makes it difficult to identify which text governs which behavior and increases the risk of accidental conflicts when editing.

options.text: Separating role, task, constraints, and output format into clear sections
options.explanation: Structured prompts with named sections make it easier to update a single concern—such as adding a new constraint—without accidentally altering unrelated instructions, and they are easier to review in code.

options.text: Changing the output schema for every request
options.explanation: Changing the output schema per request breaks downstream consumers that depend on a predictable response structure.

options.text: Hiding critical rules inside retrieved context
options.explanation: Hiding critical rules in retrieved context makes their presence uncertain; if retrieval misses a chunk, the rule is silently absent from the prompt.

questions.stem: Which tool design choice most helps an agent recover after a failed call?

options.text: Returning error details and guidance that distinguish retryable from non-retryable failures
options.explanation: A structured error response that includes error type (e.g., VALIDATION_ERROR vs. RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED) and a suggested action (e.g., fix parameter X, retry after N seconds) allows the model to choose an appropriate recovery path without human intervention.

options.text: Removing all validation from the tool
options.explanation: Removing validation means the tool accepts malformed inputs silently, making failures harder to diagnose and increasing the chance of corrupted or unexpected state changes downstream.

options.text: Always returning success even when nothing happened
options.explanation: Returning spurious success when the operation did not complete causes the agent to assume its goal was achieved and skip necessary retries, leading to silent data loss or incomplete task execution.

options.text: Forcing the user to debug raw stack traces
options.explanation: Raw stack traces expose implementation details that are not useful to the model and may include sensitive information; error responses should be model-interpretable, not developer-level debugging output.

questions.stem: An engineering team is designing an agent that must log conversation state for compliance and also support fast in-session recall. Select TWO storage choices that BEST serve these dual needs. (Select TWO)

options.text: Write structured conversation state to a Delta table for durable, auditable storage
options.explanation: Delta tables provide ACID transactions, time-travel, and audit history, satisfying compliance requirements for durable conversation logs.

options.text: Use a low-latency key-value cache such as Redis for fast in-session state reads
options.explanation: A low-latency cache provides sub-millisecond reads of recent session state, keeping agent response times fast without re-querying the Delta table on every turn.

options.text: Embed the full conversation history in the model weights through fine-tuning after each session
options.explanation: Fine-tuning the model after every session is prohibitively slow, expensive, and does not support real-time state updates.

options.text: Store everything in a temporary notebook variable that is cleared when the cluster restarts
options.explanation: Notebook variables are ephemeral and cleared at cluster restart, providing no durability for compliance logging.

questions.stem: An agent is deployed to production and the team wants to detect prompt injection attacks where adversarial text in retrieved documents attempts to override system instructions. Which defense is MOST effective?

options.text: Clearly delimit retrieved content from instructions in the prompt and add input validation that flags or strips instruction-override patterns in retrieved text
options.explanation: Structural prompt delimiting (e.g., XML tags or explicit section markers) helps the model distinguish instructions from data, and input validation to detect and strip injection patterns in retrieved text provides a pre-insertion defense layer.

options.text: Increase the model temperature to randomize behavior and reduce attack predictability
options.explanation: Higher temperature increases output randomness, which does not prevent the model from following injected instructions and may introduce other quality issues.

options.text: Store all documents in a SQL table and never embed them in the prompt to avoid retrieval entirely
options.explanation: Storing documents in SQL without embedding them defeats the purpose of RAG; context injection is required for the model to use the documents.

options.text: Require users to manually approve every retrieved chunk before it is inserted into context
options.explanation: Requiring user approval of every chunk creates extreme friction and is not practical at production scale.

questions.stem: A team evaluates their RAG agent only on final answer quality (correctness) and ignores retrieval diagnostics. What key operational risk does this create?

options.text: The team will automatically reduce storage costs by skipping retrieval logs
options.explanation: Evaluation scope has no direct effect on storage costs; retrieval logs are written regardless of whether they are analyzed.

options.text: The team cannot distinguish whether answer failures are caused by retrieval gaps or model generation errors, making targeted fixes impossible
options.explanation: Without retrieval diagnostics (precision, recall, chunk relevance scores), a team cannot tell whether low answer quality stems from retrieval returning the wrong chunks or from the model generating poor text from good chunks. Without this distinction, fixes are guesswork.

options.text: The team will achieve better tool routing performance as a side effect
options.explanation: Tool routing is unrelated to answer-only evaluation; measuring it requires separate tool-selection diagnostics.

options.text: The team can eliminate the need for any offline evaluation entirely
options.explanation: Ignoring retrieval diagnostics reduces evaluation coverage rather than replacing offline evaluation.
